sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A clinic serving adults aged 45 to 65 tested three messages encouraging colorectal cancer screening. The control group received a standard reminder. The education group received the reminder plus information about susceptibility, severity, and benefits of early detection. The education-plus-barrier group also received transportation vouchers and help scheduling appointments. After 3 months, screening completion was 36%, 52%, and 68%, respectively. Mean perceived-barrier scores on a 1 to 10 scale were 7.1, 6.2, and 3.9. The clinic noted that several patients already believed screening was important but had difficulty arranging transportation or time off work.
questions.stem: Which condition produced the highest screening completion rate?

explanations.A: Correct. The highest completion rate was 68% in the group that received education plus transportation and scheduling support. This requires using the outcome data rather than assuming information alone was sufficient. The passage also shows that the barrier score was lowest in that same group, linking the behavioral outcome to removal of practical obstacles.
explanations.B: The standard reminder group had the lowest completion rate at 36%, so it cannot be the highest-performing condition.
explanations.C: Education alone improved completion to 52%, but it still fell below the group that also received practical support.
explanations.D: The completion rates were 36%, 52%, and 68%, which are clearly not equal.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Screening completion rates and perceived-barrier scores across the three colorectal cancer screening message conditions.

questions.stem: Which Health Belief Model construct was most directly targeted by transportation vouchers and scheduling help?

explanations.B: Correct. Vouchers and scheduling assistance reduce obstacles that make the behavior difficult even when a patient sees the behavior as valuable. In the Health Belief Model, those obstacles are perceived barriers. The passage specifically states that many patients already valued screening but struggled with transportation and time off.
explanations.A: Severity refers to how serious a disease is believed to be; transportation support does not primarily make cancer seem more serious.
explanations.C: Primary appraisal is a stress-and-coping term about evaluating whether a situation is threatening.
explanations.D: Formal operational thought is a Piagetian stage, not a construct in the Health Belief Model.

questions.stem: What does the comparison between education-only and education-plus-barrier groups suggest?

choices.C: Knowledge may be necessary but not sufficient when structural barriers remain
choices.A: Education alone eliminated all access problems
choices.B: Patients did not believe screening mattered
choices.D: Barrier reduction lowered completion because it made screening less serious

explanations.C: Correct. The education group improved over control, but adding barrier reduction improved completion even more and lowered perceived-barrier scores. That pattern suggests health knowledge can help, but access obstacles may still prevent behavior change unless they are addressed directly.
explanations.A: The education-only group still had a barrier score of 6.2 and lower completion than the support group, so access problems remained.
explanations.B: The passage states that several patients already believed screening was important, so lack of belief was not the only issue.
explanations.D: Barrier reduction increased, rather than lowered, completion and did not make screening appear less serious.

questions.stem: A community program applying these findings should most likely do what?

choices.D: Combine risk education with practical support for completing screening
choices.A: Send only a one-line reminder to every patient
choices.C: Assume missed screening reflects patient irresponsibility

explanations.D: Correct. The best-performing condition addressed both cognitive factors and practical barriers. A program based on the passage should preserve the education component while also helping with transportation, scheduling, or time constraints that prevent follow-through.
explanations.A: A one-line reminder resembles the lowest-performing control condition.
explanations.B: Fear may increase concern, but the passage shows practical barriers needed direct attention.
explanations.C: The data point to structural obstacles, so blaming patients ignores the mechanism described.

questions.stem: Why can the study support stronger causal claims than a simple survey?

choices.A: Different message/support conditions were compared as interventions with measured outcomes
choices.B: Patients were grouped only by personality traits
choices.C: The study never measured screening behavior
choices.D: The passage describes only a single interview

explanations.A: Correct. The clinic compared defined intervention conditions and measured later completion, which gives more causal leverage than merely asking people about beliefs. The passage does not say the design was perfectly randomized, so the conclusion should remain focused on the intervention comparison rather than absolute certainty.
explanations.B: Personality grouping was not the design described in the passage.
explanations.C: Screening completion after 3 months was the main behavioral outcome.
explanations.D: The passage includes intervention groups and outcome data, not just one interview.

questions.passage: A beach-town health department interviewed high school students about sunscreen use. Students who intended to use sunscreen regularly described more favorable attitudes toward skin protection, stronger perceived norms from parents and teammates, and greater perceived behavioral control because sunscreen was available in their bags or locker rooms. Students who did not intend to use sunscreen often said they knew sunburns were harmful but forgot sunscreen, disliked its texture, or believed their friends would tease them. The department used the findings to revise a campaign that had previously relied only on factual information about ultraviolet radiation.
questions.stem: Which theory best organizes the three predictors described in the passage?

explanations.B: Correct. The passage centers on attitudes, subjective norms, and perceived behavioral control, the three major predictors in the theory of planned behavior. It also focuses on intention as a proximal predictor of sunscreen use, which is another key feature of that model.
explanations.A: Labeling theory concerns deviance and social labels, not attitude-norm-control predictors.
explanations.C: Gate-control theory explains pain modulation, not preventive behavior intentions.
explanations.D: James-Lange theory concerns emotion arising from bodily arousal, not sunscreen intentions.

questions.stem: A student who dislikes sunscreen texture is describing a problem with which part of the model?

explanations.C: Correct. Disliking the texture reflects an unfavorable evaluation of performing the behavior itself. In the theory of planned behavior, that is part of attitude toward the behavior, not merely social pressure or a developmental milestone.
explanations.A: Subjective norm would involve what important others approve of, such as teasing from friends.
explanations.B: Crystallized intelligence concerns accumulated knowledge and vocabulary, not sunscreen texture preferences.
explanations.D: Object permanence is an early developmental concept and does not explain the health behavior barrier.

questions.stem: Why did the old ultraviolet-radiation campaign likely miss part of the problem?

choices.D: Information about harm did not address norms, habit cues, and access
choices.A: Students were unaware that sunburns can be harmful
choices.B: Factual knowledge always prevents risky behavior
choices.C: The campaign accidentally increased sunscreen availability

explanations.D: Correct. The interviews show that many students already knew sunburns were harmful but still struggled with forgetting, texture, and peer teasing. A knowledge-only campaign would not address subjective norms or perceived control, both of which influence intention.
explanations.A: The passage says some students knew sunburns were harmful, so ignorance was not the only issue.
explanations.B: Health knowledge can help, but it does not automatically overcome social and practical barriers.
explanations.C: The passage does not say the old campaign changed availability at all.

questions.stem: Which campaign addition would most directly target perceived behavioral control?

choices.A: Putting free sunscreen dispensers in locker rooms and near beach entrances
choices.B: Listing melanoma statistics without changing access
choices.C: Telling students that sunscreen use is morally superior

explanations.A: Correct. Making sunscreen physically available at the moment of use lowers the practical effort needed to perform the behavior. That directly increases perceived behavioral control because students have easier access and fewer opportunities to forget.
explanations.B: Statistics may affect perceived risk, but they do not solve availability or forgetting.
explanations.C: Moral superiority could backfire and does not directly make the behavior easier to perform.
explanations.D: Removing reminders would weaken cues and social support rather than improve control.

questions.stem: Which finding would best show that subjective norms matter in this setting?

choices.B: Students who believed teammates approved of sunscreen reported stronger intentions even after accounting for attitudes
choices.A: Students with lockers were taller on average
choices.C: Students could define ultraviolet radiation correctly
choices.D: Sunscreen bottles had different label colors

explanations.B: Correct. If perceived approval from teammates predicts intention even when attitudes are held constant, that supports the unique role of subjective norms. It separates social approval from simply liking or disliking sunscreen.
explanations.A: Height has no direct connection to the social-norm mechanism in the passage.
explanations.C: Knowing a definition reflects knowledge, not whether important peers approve of the behavior.
explanations.D: Bottle-label color does not test the influence of perceived peer approval.
